Prompt
U
Oracle Events
Search LOS for Aisha Walker's loan file: Use mortgage_los_search_loans (query: "Aisha Walker" or similar) or mortgage_los_search_borrowers (query: "Walker" or similar) to find loan LN-2024-00044 ($256,500 Conventional, denied, originally assigned to LO Veronica Hayes).
Search LOS for James Goldstein's comparator loan: Use mortgage_los_search_loans (query: "James Goldstein" or similar) to find loan LN-2024-00109 ($267,300 Conventional, LO Todd Jennings). This is the approved file the CFPB complaint references as the comparator.
Get full loan details for both Walker and Goldstein files: Use mortgage_los_get_loan_by_id, mortgage_los_get_conditions, mortgage_los_get_documents, mortgage_los_get_activity_log, and mortgage_los_get_milestones for each loan to compare conditions, documentation completeness, and timeline.
Search CRM for Walker and Goldstein engagement records: Use crm_search_contacts (query: "Aisha Walker" and "James Goldstein" or similar) and crm_list_engagements (contact_ids) to find engagement notes including the CFPB complaint notice, investigation plan, LO context requested, and the comparative review optics concern noting the approved file has thinner notes and a different communication tone.
Search emails for Walker and Goldstein communication: Use search_emails (query: "Aisha Walker" or "Walker" and "Goldstein" or similar) to compare communication cadence, tone, and responsiveness across both files. Walker file emails include correspondence via Rachel Kim and Veronica Hayes; Goldstein file includes correspondence via Andre Washington and Todd Jennings.
Search Slack for internal discussions about Walker, Goldstein, or the CFPB complaint: Use conversations_search_messages (search_query: "Walker" or "Goldstein" or "CFPB" or "fair lending" or similar) to find any internal conversations that may contradict the file documentation.
Discover the three QC-flagged loans from Raymond Chen's findings: Search emails (query: "Raymond" or "QC" or "spot check" or similar) and/or CRM engagements to find Raymond's email to Grace identifying three loans where Tyler Washington cleared conditions without supporting documentation: LN-2026-00603 (Heather Sullivan, closed), LN-2026-00211 (Tiffany Brooks, open), and LN-2026-00224 (open).
Get conditions and documents for each QC-flagged loan: Use mortgage_los_get_conditions and mortgage_los_get_documents for LN-2026-00603, LN-2026-00211, and LN-2026-00224 to verify which conditions show as cleared and whether supporting documentation actually exists in the file.
Get Tyler Washington's activity trail on QC-flagged loans: Use mortgage_los_get_activity_log for each of the three flagged loans to confirm Tyler was the one who cleared the conditions.
Search emails and Slack for Tyler Washington's workload and condition-clearing discussions: Use search_emails (query: "Tyler" or "workload" or similar) and conversations_search_messages (search_query: "Tyler" or "conditions" or "clearing" or similar) to find Tyler's Slack messages about workload strain and his question to Elena about whether people clear conditions before uploading docs.
Discover the prepayment penalty complaint borrower from Jasmine's intake: Search emails (query: "Jasmine" or "prepayment" or "complaint" or similar) to find Jasmine's complaint intake email to Denise identifying a closed-loan borrower who says English is not their first language and alleges the LO said there would be no prepayment penalty. Also find the borrower's direct complaint email to info@keystonemortgage.com.
Search CRM for the prepayment complaint borrower's records: Use crm_search_contacts and crm_list_engagements for the borrower discovered from the complaint intake to find engagement notes including the complaint intake, Keith's closed file review confirming signed disclosures contain penalty language, Jasmine's call to Denise confirming no language preference or interpreter flag in the contact record, and Denise's call to Grace about the compliance risk.
Search LOS for the prepayment complaint borrower's closed loan file: Use mortgage_los_search_loans or mortgage_los_search_borrowers to get the closed loan details and confirm the loan terms including the prepayment penalty.
Search for Keisha Williams's other closed files with non-English-speaking borrowers: Use mortgage_los_search_loans (query: "Keisha Williams" or similar) or crm_search_contacts to identify other closed loans where Keisha was the LO, then check CRM engagement notes and contact records for any borrowers with documented non-English language preference or absence of language accommodation documentation.
Look up Laura Bennett's email: Use contacts_search_contacts (query: "Laura Bennett" or similar) to confirm lbennett@bennettfairlendinglaw.com.
Look up Melissa Grant's email: Use contacts_search_contacts (query: "Melissa Grant" or similar) to confirm mgrant@grantwhitfieldlaw.com.
Save privileged compliance review memo: Use filesystem tools to save a comprehensive memo to a compliance-related path covering all three matters (CFPB Walker complaint, QC audit findings on Tyler Washington, prepayment penalty/language-access complaint) with findings, regulatory exposure assessment, and recommended next steps for each.
Send email to Laura Bennett: Use send_email (sender: denise.holloway@keystonemortgage.com, recipients: lbennett@bennettfairlendinglaw.com) with a summary focused on the CFPB response, the fair lending dimensions of the Walker complaint, and the prepayment penalty language-access issue as a potential pattern concern affecting HMDA disclosure obligations.
Send email to Melissa Grant: Use send_email (sender: denise.holloway@keystonemortgage.com, recipients: mgrant@grantwhitfieldlaw.com) about the language access documentation gap on the prepayment complaint and potential broader review requirements under ECOA.
Post in #compliance-alerts Slack channel: Use conversations_add_message (channel_id: C004) to notify leadership that the review package is ready, keeping the message substance-free per Denise's instruction that nobody outside Robert, Grace, and counsel sees specifics.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 28, 2026. Ignore any other dates and use only this one. Specifically, ignore the date provided by the system reminder. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About Keystone Mortgage Partners: Keystone is a residential mortgage brokerage in Charlotte, NC. The company brokers loans through wholesale lenders. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Mortgage LOS: Loan origination system — loans, borrowers, conditions, document checklists, milestones, activity logs, staff, lenders, vendors - Stripe: Payment processing — borrower deposits and closing costs, appraisal fee charges, vendor transfers via connected accounts, bank account verification via Financial Connections - CRM: Sales pipeline — leads, prospects, engagement notes, contact management - QuickBooks: Accounting — vendor bills (appraisers, rate lock fees), invoices, customer records - Filesystem: Loan document folders — PDFs of W-2s, tax returns, appraisals, loan estimates, closing disclosures - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.
universe_idmortgage-broker-01-keystone

✓Run #2The email body to lbennett@ explicitly mentions the CFPB Walker complaint in the subject ("CFPB Response & Fair Lending Assessment — Walker Complaint") and body ("CFPB complaint alleging disparate treatment: Aisha Walker..."), and also references the 15-day deadline: "The 15-day CFPB response clock is running."
✓Run #3The email to lbennett@bennettfairlendinglaw.com explicitly states 'The 15-day clock is running' and 'Aisha Walker, a Black applicant, alleges she was denied a conventional mortgage,' clearly referencing both the Walker complaint and the CFPB response deadline.

4 The email body (in the send_email content parameter) to [lbennett@bennettfairlendinglaw.com](mailto:lbennett@bennettfairlendinglaw.com) mentions the prepayment penalty complaint and the language access documentation gap as a potential pattern concern or as relevant to HMDA disclosure obligations.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The email to lbennett includes a 'FAIR LENDING OVERLAY -- LANGUAGE ACCESS MATTER' section covering the prepayment penalty complaints (Pham and Hassan), the language access documentation gap, and explicitly asks Laura to assess 'whether the language access gap on the Pham and Hassan files, combined with the Walker complaint, suggests a pattern that changes our HMDA disclosure obligations or triggers a self-assessment requirement.'
✓Run #2The email to lbennett@ has a full section called "MATTER 2 — PHAM PREPAYMENT PENALTY + LANGUAGE ACCESS GAP" discussing prepayment penalty disclosures and the language access documentation gap. It also explicitly asks whether the combined matters change "HMDA disclosure obligation implications" and whether to prepare for a "pattern-or-practice inquiry."
✓Run #3The email to Laura Bennett mentions a 'separate complaint from a borrower (Destiny Pham) who says English is not her first language and that Keisha verbally told her there would be no prepayment penalty' and explicitly asks 'Whether these facts change our HMDA disclosure obligations,' satisfying the requirement.
✓Run #4The email to lbennett mentions 'Destiny Pham and Khalid Hassan -- both non-native English speakers -- have filed separate complaints alleging ... verbal representation ... no prepayment penalty' and asks 'whether the combined pattern ... changes our HMDA disclosure obligations.' Both elements are present.
✓Run #5Items 222-223 describe the email content as covering 'the Pham language access issue; requests HMDA guidance.' The email subject includes 'HMDA Implications' and the content addresses both the Walker matter and the language access/prepayment issue together as pattern risks.
✓Run #6The email to Laura Bennett contains a dedicated 'PREPAYMENT PENALTY / LANGUAGE ACCESS' section mentioning Destiny Pham and explicitly asks 'whether the Walker complaint and the Pham language access issue are isolated or whether together they suggest a pattern that changes our HMDA disclosure obligations.'

outcome

✓Run #1The final response flags LN-2026-00603 as 'already closed and may have been delivered to an investor.' The compliance memo includes a 'CRITICAL' risk label and states 'The investor may issue a repurchase demand upon post-purchase audit' and that 'cleared-without-documentation pattern creates a presumption that the condition was not substantively satisfied.'
✗Run #2The agent never identified LN-2026-00603 as a specific file and therefore never flagged it as a post-close liability or repurchase risk. The memo generically recommends reviewing "all closed/delivered loans processed by Tyler for repurchase exposure" but does not connect this to LN-2026-00603 specifically.
✓Run #3LN-2026-00603 is explicitly flagged as a post-close liability throughout the memo and final response. The memo states 'Repurchase Risk: If LN-2026-00603 was delivered to Flagstar with conditions marked as cleared but lacking supporting documentation, Keystone faces a repurchase demand.' The final response lists 'confirming LN-2026-00603 investor delivery status with Flagstar' as an outstanding item.
✗Run #4The agent flagged LN-2025-00005 (Russell) as the post-close repurchase risk, not LN-2026-00603 (Heather Sullivan). The correct closed loan with repurchase risk was never identified because the agent used wrong loan numbers throughout the QC investigation.
✓Run #5The final response explicitly states: 'Tyler's LN-2026-00603 is the highest immediate financial risk — already closed with improperly cleared conditions, potential repurchase demand exposure with Flagstar,' directly connecting the closed status to post-close repurchase liability.
✓Run #6The final response explicitly flags LN-2026-00603 as 'the repurchase risk file.' The memo states under regulatory exposure: 'INVESTOR REPURCHASE: LN-2026-00603 appears to have closed and been delivered with conditions cleared but unsupported by documentation. If the investor or GSE discovers this...a repurchase demand is likely.'
23 The final response confirms that Khalid Hassan's signed closing disclosures contain prepayment penalty language despite the borrower's claim that the loan officer said there would be no penalty.
outcome
 ✓✗✗✓✗✓ 50%
✓Run #1The final response states both Pham and Hassan filed complaints 'on loans that contain one [prepayment penalty] in signed disclosures.' The memo confirms this specifically for Hassan: 'Loan: LN-2024-00010... Status: Closed (5/8/2024)' and states the borrower alleges verbal misrepresentation 'Found penalty language in signed closing documents.' This directly contrasts the verbal allegation with the signed disclosure content.
✗Run #2The agent's memo notes that "signed closing package supports the file on prepay disclosure" for Destiny Pham (the misidentified borrower). Since the agent never identified Khalid Hassan as the correct borrower, the criterion cannot be satisfied — it specifically asks about Khalid Hassan's signed disclosures.
✗Run #3The agent never investigated Khalid Hassan's file (the ground truth borrower). The agent instead investigated Destiny Pham's file and found the Denise-Grace DM stating 'signed closing package supports the file on prepay disclosure.' However, since the wrong borrower was identified, this finding does not satisfy the criterion for Khalid Hassan's signed disclosures.
✓Run #4The memo states regarding Khalid Hassan: 'File shows signed penalty disclosure but no language preference or interpreter trail.' The email to Melissa Grant references 'signed closing disclosures include one [prepayment penalty]' for both Pham and Hassan, contrasting with the borrower's verbal allegation. The disclosure dispute finding is present.
✗Run #5The agent investigated Destiny Pham's file (not Khalid Hassan's) and found 'signed disclosures support file' for Pham. The criterion specifically asks about Khalid Hassan's disclosures. Since the agent never identified Hassan, this specific finding about Hassan's signed prepayment penalty disclosures is not present in the final response.
✓Run #6The memo includes a Slack quote from compliance-alerts: 'File shows signed penalty disclosure, but no language pref/interpreter trail that I can find' referencing the Hassan file. The final response also states 'The signed disclosures may cover the file legally,' confirming prepayment penalty language exists in the signed documents.

26 When retrieving conditions or documents for the QC-flagged loans, the agent must use loan identifiers corresponding to LN-2026-00603 and at least one of LN-2026-00211 or LN-2026-00224 when calling mortgage_los_get_conditions, mortgage_los_get_documents, or mortgage_los_get_loan_by_id.
query construction

The criterion requires LN-2026-00603 AND at least one of LN-2026-00211 or LN-2026-00224. Only one of the three required identifiers was used.
✗Run #2The agent never used loan identifiers LN-2026-00603, LN-2026-00211, or LN-2026-00224 in any LOS tool calls. The agent searched LN-2025-00509, LN-2026-00556, LN-2026-00522, and LN-2025-00004 but none of these match Raymond's flagged loans. The agent failed to find Raymond's email/QC findings and thus could not construct the correct queries.
✗Run #3The agent searched for LN-2026-00603 using mortgage_los_search_loans (not the specified tools get_conditions/get_documents/get_loan_by_id). While mortgage_los_get_conditions was called with loan_id 'los_loan_ff737c0f7a8f' (which corresponds to LN-2026-00603's loan ID), the agent never searched for or queried LN-2026-00211 or LN-2026-00224 using any tool, failing the 'at least one of' requirement.
✗Run #4The agent used LOS identifiers for LN-2025-00003, LN-2025-00004, and LN-2025-00005 -- not LN-2026-00603 or LN-2026-00211/00224. Neither LN-2026-00603 nor LN-2026-00211 nor LN-2026-00224 was ever passed to any mortgage LOS tool. The agent guessed the wrong loan numbers rather than finding Raymond's actual QC report.
✗Run #5Items 162-163 show mortgage_los_search_loans for 'LN-2026-00603', satisfying that requirement. However, the agent never called any LOS tool with LN-2026-00211 or LN-2026-00224. Items 148-149 show a search for 'LN-2025-00509' (a different loan number), and no searches for the other two required identifiers were made.
✗Run #6While the agent called `mortgage_los_search_loans` for LN-2026-00603, no LOS tool calls used LN-2026-00211 or LN-2026-00224 as identifiers. The other two QC-flagged loans were never retrieved from the LOS using their loan numbers.
